title: AI Code Assistant Concepts
createdFor: Short instructional videos about AI code assistant concepts.
productionAssumptions: Each video is compact and visual-first.
Exact prices and product limits are treated as volatile and are not encoded on-screen.
The primary deliverable is a coherent multi-video set, not a single monolithic lecture.
sourceMaterialPolicy: Use official documentation for volatile product claims.
Use the user's report as the content outline and dependency map.
Keep reusable visual primitives consistent across all videos.

concepts.title: What Is an LLM?
concepts.objective: A large language model predicts tokens from context; it is not a database lookup.

scenes.headline: Text becomes tokens
scenes.micro: Words, spaces, punctuation, and word pieces enter the context box.

scenes.headline: The model ranks next tokens
scenes.micro: Many candidates get scores before one continuation is selected.

scenes.headline: Output loops back
scenes.micro: Each generated token becomes part of the next step's context.

scenes.headline: Size is not the same as truth
scenes.micro: Parameters, context window, training data, and verification are different concerns.

scenes.headline: Better context changes odds
scenes.micro: Clearer task context reshapes the token distribution.

concepts.title: LLM Billing
concepts.objective: Cost follows model work, even when a product wraps usage as seats, credits, or subscriptions.

scenes.headline: The same work can wear different price labels
scenes.micro: Subscription, credit, API, and local costs are packaging around real compute.

scenes.headline: Input and output both matter
scenes.micro: Long prompts, long answers, and repeated tool loops add up.

scenes.headline: Model choice changes the meter
scenes.micro: A stronger model can cost more but may reduce retries on hard work.

scenes.headline: Cache and trim before scaling
scenes.micro: Repeated context should be cached or summarized when the harness supports it.

scenes.headline: Track cost per accepted result
scenes.micro: The useful metric is completed work, not one isolated call.

concepts.title: Probabilities and Evaluation
concepts.objective: LLM output is sampled from probabilities; evaluation decides whether the sample is good enough.

scenes.headline: Likely is not guaranteed
scenes.micro: The model scores candidate continuations instead of fetching one fixed answer.

scenes.headline: Context reshapes the distribution
scenes.micro: Examples and constraints can move probability toward useful outputs.

scenes.headline: Tests beat vibes when possible
scenes.micro: Schemas, unit tests, parsers, and exact checks make quality measurable.

scenes.headline: pass@N trades attempts for cost
scenes.micro: More samples can improve success, but every attempt consumes time and budget.

scenes.headline: Agent evals need traces
scenes.micro: For multi-step work, grade the path as well as the final answer.

concepts.title: What Is an Agent?
concepts.objective: An agent is a goal-directed loop that observes, acts, reads results, and continues until it should stop.

scenes.headline: A prompt answers; an agent pursues
scenes.micro: The system can choose steps after seeing new information.

scenes.headline: Context defines what it sees
scenes.micro: Files, chat history, tool output, and rules become the working state.

scenes.headline: Tools define what it can change
scenes.micro: Reading, running, editing, searching, and calling APIs create real effects.

scenes.headline: Stop conditions keep work bounded
scenes.micro: The harness must know when to finish, ask, or hand off.

scenes.headline: Autonomy requires controls
scenes.micro: Permissions, guardrails, and evaluation rise in importance as loops get longer.

concepts.title: What Is a Guardrail?
concepts.objective: A guardrail enforces policy around inputs, outputs, or actions.

scenes.headline: Prompting asks; guardrails enforce
scenes.micro: Policy checks can block, redact, route, or require review.

scenes.headline: Check ingress and egress separately
scenes.micro: Incoming prompts, tool results, final output, and actions have different risks.

scenes.headline: Protect secrets and destructive actions
scenes.micro: Secret scans and approval gates are pragmatic first controls.

scenes.headline: Precision matters
scenes.micro: A broad block can stop useful work; a narrow block can miss risk.

scenes.headline: Log decisions for improvement
scenes.micro: Guardrail events become evidence for tuning policy.

concepts.title: What Is a Harness?
concepts.objective: A harness is the runtime shell that turns a raw model into a usable assistant or agent.

scenes.headline: The product is not just the model
scenes.micro: Instructions, tools, permissions, memory, and defaults shape behavior.

scenes.headline: Instruction layers set priorities
scenes.micro: System, organization, repository, and user rules can compete.

scenes.headline: The harness mediates actions
scenes.micro: A model proposes; the runtime approves, executes, observes, or blocks.

scenes.headline: Defaults change cost and risk
scenes.micro: More context, more tools, and more autonomy can mean more spend and exposure.

scenes.headline: Choose the shell for the job
scenes.micro: Workflow fit matters more than a feature checklist.

concepts.title: What Is a Harness Hook?
concepts.objective: A hook is an event-driven interception point inside the harness lifecycle.

scenes.headline: An event fires
scenes.micro: Session start, prompt submit, pre-tool use, permission request, and stop events are common.

scenes.headline: Custom logic inspects the payload
scenes.micro: A hook can validate, log, transform, notify, approve, or deny.

scenes.headline: Hooks turn policy into execution
scenes.micro: They are useful for secrets, destructive commands, formatting, and context shaping.

scenes.headline: Latency is part of the design
scenes.micro: Slow or over-broad hooks can become a hidden tax.

scenes.headline: Package useful hooks for reuse
scenes.micro: A hook pattern that works once often belongs in a plugin or skill.

concepts.title: What Is a Harness Plugin?
concepts.objective: A plugin packages reusable harness behavior so teams can install and govern it.

scenes.headline: Plugins turn setup into a package
scenes.micro: Instead of copying local files, a team installs a named bundle.

scenes.headline: Bundles can contain runtime pieces
scenes.micro: Skills, hooks, agents, MCP servers, tools, and settings may travel together.

scenes.headline: Manifest plus permissions
scenes.micro: Discovery should not imply unlimited access.

scenes.headline: Distribution is governance
scenes.micro: Versioning, review, source trust, and rollback matter.

scenes.headline: Good plugins reduce drift
scenes.micro: Shared defaults make teams more consistent and less repetitive.

concepts.title: What Is a Skill?
concepts.objective: A skill is on-demand domain expertise: instructions, resources, and scripts for a repeatable task.

scenes.headline: Stop pasting the same checklist
scenes.micro: Name the workflow and make it discoverable.

scenes.headline: Keep the core concise
scenes.micro: A short SKILL file should route to references only when needed.

scenes.headline: Progressive disclosure saves context
scenes.micro: Large details stay available without entering every prompt.

scenes.headline: Scripts make the process repeatable
scenes.micro: Deterministic helpers reduce copying mistakes and variance.

scenes.headline: Validate the skill alone
scenes.micro: A good skill should work in isolation, not only with hidden repo memory.

concepts.title: What Is MCP?
concepts.objective: Model Context Protocol is standardized plumbing between AI clients and external tools or data.

scenes.headline: MCP replaces bespoke glue
scenes.micro: Clients and servers meet through a shared protocol.

scenes.headline: Servers expose capabilities
scenes.micro: Tools, resources, and prompts become discoverable.

scenes.headline: The host still controls policy
scenes.micro: Authentication, authorization, and approval are separate design decisions.

scenes.headline: Tool sprawl creates cost and risk
scenes.micro: Every exposed tool can add context, decision load, and attack surface.

scenes.headline: Start narrow and read-only
scenes.micro: Expand write capability only after approval and auditing are clear.

concepts.title: AI Alternatives
concepts.objective: Choose assistants by workflow gravity: where context lives, what actions matter, and how control works.

scenes.headline: There is no universal winner
scenes.micro: Compare the job, not only the model name.

scenes.headline: Products have centers of gravity
scenes.micro: Knowledge work, coding, productivity, research, and custom harnesses differ.

scenes.headline: Use the same checklist
scenes.micro: Context, tools, permissions, evaluation, billing, and governance.

scenes.headline: Pilot with real tasks
scenes.micro: Measure accepted results, corrections, latency, and permission friction.

scenes.headline: Live plan facts change
scenes.micro: Keep exact prices and limits in source notes, not in evergreen animation.

concepts.title: Observability
concepts.objective: Observability makes agent behavior inspectable through traces, spans, events, costs, and outcomes.

scenes.headline: You cannot improve what you cannot inspect
scenes.micro: Agent failures hide inside prompts, tool calls, and retries.

scenes.headline: Record the run tree
scenes.micro: Model calls, tool calls, inputs, outputs, latency, and cost belong together.

scenes.headline: Grade traces, not only answers
scenes.micro: A correct final result can still take a risky or wasteful path.

scenes.headline: Find regressions over time
scenes.micro: Compare success rate, drift, error causes, and budget by version.

scenes.headline: Feed learning back into skills and hooks
scenes.micro: Reusable fixes should become runtime guidance, not tribal memory.

concepts.title: Instruction Layers
concepts.objective: Instruction layers are the rule stack that shapes behavior before the model sees the task.

scenes.headline: Instructions have precedence
scenes.micro: System, organization, project, user, and skill instructions do not all weigh the same.

scenes.headline: Conflicts must be explicit
scenes.micro: If rules disagree, the harness needs a deterministic priority order.

scenes.headline: Local rules encode local reality
scenes.micro: Repo conventions, deploy rules, test commands, and style constraints belong near the work.

scenes.headline: Too many rules create fog
scenes.micro: Long stale instruction files cost attention and can misroute the agent.

scenes.headline: Promote stable patterns into skills
scenes.micro: Repeated procedural guidance should become a scoped capability.

concepts.title: Tools and Permissions
concepts.objective: Tool permission is the boundary between helpful suggestion and real-world action.

scenes.headline: Tools change the environment
scenes.micro: Read, write, run, browse, deploy, and message tools have different risk.

scenes.headline: Default to least privilege
scenes.micro: Give the agent the smallest tool surface that can complete the task.

scenes.headline: Approve high-impact actions
scenes.micro: Deletes, production writes, payments, and external messages need checkpoints.

scenes.headline: Permission prompts need context
scenes.micro: Humans approve better when they see command, target, reason, and blast radius.

scenes.headline: Audit what actually happened
scenes.micro: Tool history turns permission policy into reviewable evidence.

concepts.title: Data Governance
concepts.objective: Data governance decides what the assistant may see, retain, train on, and reveal.

scenes.headline: Context is data movement
scenes.micro: Every prompt, file, connector, and tool result moves information across a boundary.

scenes.headline: Permissions should follow the user
scenes.micro: The agent should not access more than the person it represents.

scenes.headline: Retention and training policies matter
scenes.micro: Teams need to know what is logged, stored, retained, or used for improvement.

scenes.headline: Connectors inherit source-system risk
scenes.micro: A badly scoped integration can leak data even if the model is well behaved.

scenes.headline: Governance is part of product selection
scenes.micro: Choose tools whose controls match your regulatory and operational needs.

sources.label: GitHub Copilot billing and AI credits
sources.note: Usage-based billing converts model-priced token usage into AI credits; plan details change over time.

sources.label: GitHub Copilot agent skills
sources.note: Skills are reusable instruction and resource folders for agent workflows.

sources.label: Claude Code skills
sources.note: Skills load task-specific instructions and resources on demand.

sources.label: Claude Code hooks
sources.note: Hooks run commands, HTTP endpoints, or LLM prompts at lifecycle events.

sources.label: Claude Code plugins
sources.note: Plugins can package skills, agents, hooks, MCP servers, and related runtime pieces.

sources.label: Model Context Protocol introduction
sources.note: MCP standardizes connections between AI applications and external tools or data.

sources.label: Google Cloud Model Armor
sources.note: Model Armor screens prompts and responses for AI security risks.

sources.label: Atlassian Rovo usage limits
sources.note: Rovo exposes a product-specific usage and credit model.

sources.label: Google AI plans
sources.note: Gemini product access and plan limits vary by plan and region.

sources.label: OpenAI Evals guide
sources.note: Evaluation is built from datasets, graders, traces, and quality measurements.

sources.label: Anthropic effective agents
sources.note: Agentic systems differ from fixed workflows by dynamically directing tool use.
